flashcards.front: What is the difference between a class and an object in C++?
flashcards.back: A class is a blueprint or template defining properties (data members) and behaviors (member functions) of a type. An object is a concrete instance of a class that occupies memory; multiple objects can be created from a single class.

flashcards.front: What is the 'protected' access specifier in C++?
flashcards.back: Protected members are accessible within the class and by derived classes but not from outside the class. It provides an intermediate level between private (most restrictive) and public, enabling inheritance while maintaining encapsulation.

flashcards.front: What are the different types of constructors in C++?
flashcards.back: Default constructor (no parameters), Parameterized constructor (accepts arguments), Copy constructor (creates object from another object), and Move constructor (C++11). Constructors have the same name as the class and no return type.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of a destructor in C++?
flashcards.back: A destructor is a special member function automatically called when an object goes out of scope or is deleted. It releases resources (memory, file handles) allocated by the object; has the same name as class prefixed with tilde (~), no return type or parameters.

flashcards.front: What is the 'this' pointer in C++?
flashcards.back: 'this' is an implicit pointer pointing to the current object instance, automatically passed to all non-static member functions. It distinguishes between object parameters and class data members with identical names, and returns the current object from member functions.

flashcards.front: What are static members in C++?
flashcards.back: Static data members belong to the class itself rather than any object, shared among all instances. Static member functions can be called without an object using the scope resolution operator (::) but can only access static data members and other static functions.

flashcards.front: What is a friend function in C++?
flashcards.back: A friend function is a non-member function granted special access to private and protected members of a class, declared inside the class using the 'friend' keyword. It is useful for operator overloading and allowing special functions to access internal state without breaking encapsulation.

flashcards.front: What is a const member function in C++?
flashcards.back: A const member function guarantees it will not modify the object's state, declared with 'const' after the function signature. Const objects can only call const member functions; this provides read-only access and improves code safety and clarity.
